content.text: Azure organizes billing and purchasing through distinct account types, each suited to a different customer size and procurement model. Choosing the right account type determines pricing, billing mechanics, support entitlements, and how subscriptions are governed across an organization.

attrs.alt: Azure account types comparison: Individual/PAYG vs Enterprise Agreement vs CSP vs MCA

content.text: The default account type created during standard sign-up. Usage is billed monthly to a credit card at list prices. There is no commitment, no minimum spend, and no volume discounts.
Suitable for: individuals, students, developers, startups, and small teams with unpredictable spend.
Billing: monthly invoice to a payment method on file (credit card or bank account via ACH in some regions).
Pricing: Microsoft published list prices with no discount. Azure Reservations and Savings Plans can reduce costs.
Support: Basic support included; Developer, Standard, Professional Direct, and Premier support are add-ons.
Governance: single subscription owner manages access; no enterprise-level policy tooling required.

# Verify account type (quotaId identifies the offer)
az rest --method GET \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>?api-version=2022-12-01' \
  --query 'subscriptionPolicies.quotaId'

# Common quotaId values:
# 'PayAsYouGo_2014-09-01'  -> Standard PAYG
# 'FreeTrial'               -> Free Account (30-day trial)
# 'AzurePass_2014-09-01'   -> Azure Pass / Partner-sponsored
# 'MSDN_2014-09-01'        -> Visual Studio subscriber benefit
# 'MSDNDevTest_2014-09-01' -> Visual Studio Dev/Test pricing

# List all subscriptions and their offer types
az account list --all --query '[].{Name:name, State:state, Id:id}' -o table

The Enterprise Agreement is Microsoft's volume licensing program for large organizations. It requires a minimum annual Azure commitment of approximately $100,000 USD and is negotiated directly with Microsoft or through a Large Account Reseller (LAR). EA customers receive negotiated discounts, a dedicated Microsoft account team, and access to EA-specific portal features.
~$100,000 USD/year in Azure consumption (varies by region)
Annual or upfront invoice; Azure Prepayment (formerly Monetary Commitment)
Negotiated percentage off list prices, typically 10–20%
Azure Dev/Test offers reduced pricing on Windows VMs for MSDN subscribers
ea.azure.com — enrollment management, cost reports, department/account structure
EA hierarchy: Enrollment → Department → Account → Subscription
Unified Enterprise Support recommended; negotiated SLA

# EA subscriptions appear alongside PAYG subscriptions in az account list
az account list --query '[].{Name:name,Id:id,Tenant:tenantId}' -o table

# EA billing account details (requires Billing Reader or higher)
az billing account list -o table

# List EA enrollment accounts
az billing enrollment-account list -o table

# Create a new subscription under an EA enrollment account
az account create \
  --display-name 'prod-platform-sub' \
  --billing-account-name <enrollment-id> \
  --billing-profile-name <billing-profile> \
  --invoice-section-name <invoice-section> \
  --workload Production

# Query EA usage (requires Enterprise Reader role on the enrollment)
az consumption usage list \
  --billing-period-name <YYYYMM> \
  --query '[].{Resource:instanceName,Cost:pretaxCost}' -o table | head -20

EA customers use a separate portal (ea.azure.com) for enrollment management, budget allocation, and department cost reports. The Azure portal (portal.azure.com) is used for resource management. Both portals share the same subscriptions but provide different administrative views.

The CSP program allows Microsoft partners (resellers, managed service providers, system integrators) to sell Azure subscriptions to their customers. The customer purchases Azure through the partner rather than directly from Microsoft. The partner handles billing, support, and often manages resources on the customer's behalf.
Customer receives a single monthly invoice from the partner, not from Microsoft directly.
Partner can bundle Azure usage with managed services, consulting, and support into a single bill.
Two CSP models: Direct (partner bills Microsoft directly) and Indirect (partner works through a distributor).
Customers cannot independently contact Microsoft for billing support — the partner is the first point of contact.
Azure pricing to the customer is set by the partner, who purchases from Microsoft at partner pricing.
CSP subscriptions are linked to the partner's tenant; customer has delegated administration rights.

# CSP subscriptions are managed via Partner Center (partner.microsoft.com)
# From the Azure CLI, CSP subscriptions appear as normal subscriptions:
az account list --all -o table

# CSP tenants: the partner's tenant delegates access to the customer tenant
# Check if a subscription is CSP via the quotaId:
az rest --method GET \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>?api-version=2022-12-01' \
  --query 'subscriptionPolicies.quotaId'
# CSP quotaId values start with 'CSPARM' or 'CSP'

# Partner admin link (PAL) — associates partner with customer subscription for credit
az managementpartner create --partner-id <MPN-partner-id>
az managementpartner show

The Microsoft Customer Agreement is a modern, paperless purchasing agreement that replaces the older Microsoft Online Subscription Agreement (MOSA) for PAYG customers and certain EA renewals. MCA is the default agreement for new direct customers and is also available as an indirect path through CSP partners.
Billing account → Billing profile → Invoice section → Subscription

MCA introduces a new billing hierarchy: a Billing Account contains one or more Billing Profiles (each with its own invoice and payment method), and each Billing Profile contains Invoice Sections (cost centers or teams). Subscriptions are linked to Invoice Sections.

# MCA billing account structure
# List billing accounts (includes MCA accounts)
az billing account list -o table

# List billing profiles under an MCA account
az billing profile list \
  --account-name <billing-account-id> \
  -o table

# List invoice sections under a billing profile
az billing invoice section list \
  --account-name <billing-account-id> \
  --profile-name <billing-profile-id> \
  -o table

# Create a subscription under an MCA invoice section
az account create \
  --display-name 'engineering-prod' \
  --billing-account-name <billing-account-id> \
  --billing-profile-name <billing-profile-id> \
  --invoice-section-name <invoice-section-id> \
  --workload Production

# List all subscriptions under a billing profile
az billing subscription list \
  --account-name <billing-account-id> \
  --profile-name <billing-profile-id> \
  -o table

MOSA is the legacy click-through agreement used by most PAYG subscriptions created before MCA became the default. Each subscription has its own independent agreement. Microsoft is gradually migrating MOSA customers to MCA.
One agreement per subscription — no shared billing hierarchy.
Billing is per-subscription to a single payment method.
Cannot share reserved instances or savings plans across subscriptions as easily as MCA.
Microsoft's Cost Management tools work, but cross-subscription views require Management Groups rather than billing profiles.
New subscriptions created today default to MCA in most regions.

Dev/Test subscription types offer reduced pricing on Windows and SQL Server VMs for active Visual Studio subscribers. They are intended for development and testing, not production workloads — running production services on Dev/Test subscriptions violates the license terms.
Pay Linux rates for Windows VMs
VS subscribers within an EA enrollment

# Create a Dev/Test subscription (workload = DevTest)
az account create \
  --display-name 'engineering-devtest' \
  --billing-account-name <billing-account-id> \
  --billing-profile-name <billing-profile-id> \
  --invoice-section-name <invoice-section-id> \
  --workload DevTest

# Verify the workload type
az account show --query '{Name:name, Id:id}' -o json

# Dev/Test VMs: pricing uses Linux rates for Windows; verify with pricing calculator
az vm list-skus --location eastus \
  --query '[?name==`Standard_D2s_v5`].{Name:name,Family:family}' -o table
Flexibility; Savings Plans reduce cost without commitment
Consolidated billing, modern agreement, no minimum commit
Negotiated discounts, dedicated support, EA portal
SMB using a managed service provider
Partner bundles Azure with management services
Special pricing via Microsoft's social programs
Reduced Windows VM pricing for VS subscribers

Account type decisions made early are hard to reverse. Migrating from PAYG to EA requires a new enrollment and subscription transfer. Migrating from CSP to direct requires partner coordination. Evaluate the expected spend trajectory for the next 12–24 months before signing any committed agreement.
EA customers manage Azure through a four-level hierarchy within the EA portal. Understanding this hierarchy is essential for setting up cost governance and delegating access to business units.

# --- EA: View enrollment billing accounts ---
az billing account list \
  --query '[?agreementType==`EnterpriseAgreement`].{Name:displayName, Id:name}' \
  -o table

# --- EA: List all subscriptions under the enrollment ---
az billing subscription list \
  --account-name <enrollment-billing-account-id> \
  --query '[].{Name:displayName,State:subscriptionBillingStatus,Id:subscriptionId}' \
  -o table

# --- EA: View department (billing profile equivalent) costs ---
az consumption budget list \
  --query '[].{Name:name,Amount:amount,CurrentSpend:currentSpend.amount}' \
  -o table

# --- EA: Check Azure Prepayment (Monetary Commitment) balance ---
az billing balance show \
  --account-name <enrollment-id> \
  --billing-period-name <YYYYMM> \
  --query '{Commitment:beginningBalance,Used:totalUsage,Remaining:endingBalance}'
MCA introduces a three-level billing hierarchy below the Billing Account. This enables organizations to separate invoices by division and cost centers without needing separate billing accounts.

# --- MCA: Full hierarchy exploration ---

# Step 1: Get the MCA billing account ID
BILLING_ACCOUNT=$(az billing account list \
  --query '[?agreementType==`MicrosoftCustomerAgreement`].name' \
  -o tsv | head -1)
echo "Billing account: $BILLING_ACCOUNT"

# Step 2: List billing profiles (separate invoices/payment methods)
az billing profile list \
  --account-name $BILLING_ACCOUNT \
  --query '[].{Name:displayName,Id:name,Currency:currency}' \
  -o table

# Step 3: List invoice sections under each profile (cost centers/teams)
PROFILE=$(az billing profile list \
  --account-name $BILLING_ACCOUNT \
  --query '[0].name' -o tsv)

az billing invoice section list \
  --account-name $BILLING_ACCOUNT \
  --profile-name $PROFILE \
  --query '[].{Section:displayName,Id:name}' \
  -o table

# Step 4: List subscriptions linked to an invoice section
SECTION=$(az billing invoice section list \
  --account-name $BILLING_ACCOUNT \
  --profile-name $PROFILE \
  --query '[0].name' -o tsv)

az billing subscription list \
  --account-name $BILLING_ACCOUNT \
  --profile-name $PROFILE \
  --invoice-section-name $SECTION \
  -o table

Subscriptions can be transferred between billing accounts when organizations change procurement models — for example, converting a PAYG subscription to EA, or moving from one CSP partner to another. Transfers do not interrupt running resources.
PAYG to EA: Initiate from the EA portal (ea.azure.com) using the 'Transfer Subscription' feature. Requires Owner role on the subscription.
PAYG/EA to MCA: Use the Azure portal Billing blade to initiate. Microsoft sends the original owner an email to accept.
Between CSP partners: Must go through Microsoft Partner Center. The current CSP partner must consent to the transfer.
EA to CSP: Requires the EA enrollment to permit CSP transfers and the new CSP partner to set up the relationship.
Subscription transfer preserves resources, RBAC assignments, and Azure Policy assignments. Billing history stays with the original account.

# Initiate a subscription transfer request (MCA context)
# The transfer is done via the Azure Portal -> Billing -> Subscriptions -> Transfer
# CLI equivalent: create a transfer request
az billing transfer initiate \
  --account-name <target-billing-account> \
  --profile-name <target-billing-profile> \
  --invoice-section-name <target-invoice-section> \
  --subscription-ids <sub-id-1> <sub-id-2>

# Check transfer status
az billing transfer show \
  --account-name <target-billing-account> \
  --profile-name <target-billing-profile> \
  --invoice-section-name <target-invoice-section> \
  --transfer-name <transfer-id>

# After transfer: verify the subscription now appears under the new billing account
az account show --subscription <sub-id> \
  --query '{Name:name, Id:id, Tenant:tenantId}'

Billing account types each have their own RBAC model for the billing plane, separate from Azure resource RBAC. A user can have billing read access without having any Azure resource access, and vice versa.
Full or read-only access to all billing profiles and invoice sections
Billing Profile Owner, Contributor, Reader, Invoice Manager
Billing profile-level: download invoices, manage payment methods
Invoice Section Owner, Contributor, Reader, Azure Subscription Creator
Invoice section-level: create subscriptions, manage costs
Enterprise Administrator, Department Administrator, Account Owner, Read Only
Enrollment or department-level cost visibility and management

# MCA: Assign Billing Account Reader (view costs, no resource access)
az billing role assignment create \
  --account-name <billing-account-id> \
  --role-definition-name 'Billing account reader' \
  --user-email-address finance-team@company.com

# MCA: List existing billing role assignments
az billing role assignment list \
  --account-name <billing-account-id> \
  --query '[].{Principal:userEmailAddress,Role:roleDefinitionId}' \
  -o table

# MCA: Grant Azure Subscription Creator role on an invoice section
# (allows creating new subscriptions without Billing Account Owner)
az billing role assignment create \
  --account-name <billing-account-id> \
  --profile-name <billing-profile-id> \
  --invoice-section-name <invoice-section-id> \
  --role-definition-name 'Azure subscription creator' \
  --user-email-address devlead@company.com

# EA: List EA administrator assignments
az billing role assignment list \
  --account-name <ea-enrollment-id> \
  -o table
Cost Savings Instruments Available by Account Type
Azure offers several instruments to reduce costs beyond negotiated EA discounts. Availability varies by account type.
Up to 72% discount on VMs, SQL, Cosmos DB
Up to 65% discount on compute, flexible across VM families
Use on-prem licenses on Azure; up to 85% savings on Windows
Up to 90% discount on interruptible compute
Linux rates for Windows VMs in dev/test

# Apply Azure Hybrid Benefit to a Windows VM (uses your on-prem Windows license)
az vm update \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --name vm-prod-01 \
  --license-type Windows_Server

# Verify hybrid benefit is applied
az vm show \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --name vm-prod-01 \
  --query 'licenseType'

# List all VMs not using Hybrid Benefit (potential savings)
az vm list --query '[?storageProfile.osDisk.osType==`Windows` && licenseType!=`Windows_Server`].{Name:name,RG:resourceGroup}' -o table

# Create a reservation (1-year, D2s_v5, eastus, 2 instances)
az reservations reservation-order purchase \
  --reservation-order-id $(uuidgen) \
  --display-name 'D2sv5-prod-1yr' \
  --applied-scope-type Shared \
  --billing-scope /subscriptions/<sub-id> \
  --term P1Y \
  --billing-plan Upfront \
  --quantity 2 \
  --sku-name Standard_D2s_v5 \
  --location eastus
Managing Multiple Subscriptions Across Account Types

Large organizations often have subscriptions under multiple account types simultaneously — for example, an EA enrollment for production, a PAYG subscription for R&D, and a Dev/Test subscription for engineering. Managing these from a single pane requires Management Groups and appropriate billing RBAC.

# List all subscriptions across all account types in the tenant
az account list --all \
  --query '[].{Name:name,State:state,Id:id,Tenant:tenantId}' \
  -o table

# Group subscriptions under a management group for unified policy/RBAC
az account management-group create \
  --name mg-all-production \
  --display-name 'All Production Subscriptions'

# Move PAYG and EA subscriptions into the same management group
az account management-group subscription add \
  --name mg-all-production \
  --subscription <payg-sub-id>

az account management-group subscription add \
  --name mg-all-production \
  --subscription <ea-sub-id>

# Apply a policy at the management group level (spans all subscription types)
az policy assignment create \
  --name require-tags-all \
  --policy /providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/<policy-def-id> \
  --scope /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-all-production

# View all resources across all subscriptions (requires Reader on MG)
az resource list \
  --query 'length(@)' \
  -o tsv
Identifying Account Type via Azure REST API

Programmatic checks against the subscription properties let automation scripts behave differently based on the billing account type — for example, skipping EA-specific budget APIs for PAYG subscriptions.

# Detect account type from subscription metadata
az rest --method GET \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>?api-version=2022-12-01' \
  --query '{QuotaId:subscriptionPolicies.quotaId, SpendingLimit:subscriptionPolicies.spendingLimit}'

# Mapping of quotaId to account type:
#   FreeTrial                -> Azure Free Account
#   PayAsYouGo_2014-09-01    -> Standard PAYG
#   MSDN_2014-09-01          -> Visual Studio subscriber (PAYG rates + benefit)
#   MSDNDevTest_2014-09-01   -> Visual Studio Dev/Test pricing
#   EnterpriseAgreement_*    -> EA enrollment
#   EA_*                     -> EA (older format)
#   MCA_2019-*               -> Microsoft Customer Agreement
#   CSPARM_*                 -> Cloud Solution Provider

# Shell script: check and branch on account type
QUOTA=$(az rest --method GET \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>?api-version=2022-12-01' \
  --query 'subscriptionPolicies.quotaId' -o tsv)

case $QUOTA in
  *EnterpriseAgreement*|*EA_*)
    echo 'EA subscription: using enrollment billing APIs'
    ;;
  *MCA*)
    echo 'MCA subscription: using billing profile APIs'
    ;;
  *CSP*|*CSPARM*)
    echo 'CSP subscription: contact partner for billing details'
    ;;
  PayAsYouGo*)
    echo 'PAYG subscription: standard cost management APIs'
    ;;
  FreeTrial)
    echo 'Free trial: spending limit active, upgrade before production'
    ;;
  *)
    echo "Unknown quota type: $QUOTA"
    ;;
esac
Default support entitlements vary significantly by account type. EA and large MCA customers can negotiate premium support as part of the agreement.

EA customers should negotiate Unified Enterprise support as part of the EA enrollment rather than purchasing Standard or ProDirect separately — it typically costs less at scale and provides on-site support options unavailable in other tiers.

# Check current support plan (requires billing reader or support contributor)
az support services list -o table

# Create a support ticket via CLI (works on any account type)
az support tickets create \
  --ticket-name 'billing-question-001' \
  --title 'Question about EA invoice charges' \
  --description 'We see unexpected charges on our EA invoice for month YYYY-MM' \
  --problem-classification '/providers/Microsoft.Support/services/billing-service/problemClassifications/billing-issue' \
  --severity moderate \
  --contact-first-name 'Alice' \
  --contact-last-name 'Smith' \
  --contact-email alice@company.com \
  --contact-country US \
  --contact-preferred-support-language en-US

# List open support tickets
az support tickets list --query '[?status==`open`].{Id:name,Title:title,Severity:severity}' -o table

Azure offers four primary account types: Pay-As-You-Go for individuals and small teams with no commitment; Enterprise Agreement for large organizations with negotiated discounts and a $100K/year minimum; Cloud Solution Provider for customers purchasing through Microsoft partners; and Microsoft Customer Agreement for a modern, paperless agreement with a flexible billing hierarchy. Dev/Test subscriptions are available under all agreement types to provide reduced pricing for non-production workloads. The billing account type determines the `quotaId` visible on each subscription and governs what billing APIs and portal features are available.
